title: Contractor vs Employee Salary Comparison
description: Compare a full-time salary against a specific hourly contract rate to see which actually pays more.
methodSummary: True Equivalent = Contract Gross minus the Self-Employment Overhead %.
assumptions: The overhead percentage accounts for self-employment tax, missed retirement matching, absence of PTO, and health insurance costs.
Calculates comparative gross equivalence. Exact net tax outcomes may slightly differ.
insights: The most common mistake contractors make is setting their overhead percent to 0. A safe baseline is 30% to account for everything a salaried role provides implicitly.
If the 'Difference %' is less than +15%, the stability of the employee role might make it more valuable than the contract.

faqs.q: What should I enter for Self-Employment Overhead?
faqs.a: Usually 20-30%. This accounts for self-employment taxes (approx 15.3% in the US), healthcare premiums, skipped pension contributions, and unpaid sick days.

faqs.q: Why is 'Contractor True Equivalent' lower than 'Gross Revenue'?
faqs.a: Because Gross Revenue is business cash flow, not your salary. The True Equivalent backs out the business costs your employer normally pays.

faqs.q: Do I need to account for software or hardware expenses?
faqs.a: Yes. If you must buy your own laptop and software licenses as a contractor, mentally raise the overhead percentage to compensate.

faqs.q: Why is standard salaried PTO omitted from the employee side?
faqs.a: Because the employee receives the full salary regardless of taking the 4 weeks PTO. The contractor loses billable hours when taking PTO, which is handled via 'Weeks/Year'.

faqs.q: If the difference is negative, should I avoid contracting?
faqs.a: Mathematically yes. A negative difference means you are taking on all the business risk of contracting while effectively taking a pay cut.

faqs.q: Does this tool work for international remote jobs?
faqs.a: Yes, as long as you factor the local self-employment compliance costs into the overhead percentage precisely.

richContent:
Contractor vs Employee Comparison

Factor Employee (W-2) Contractor (1099)
Taxes (US) Employer pays half FICA You pay full 15.3% Self-Employment Tax
PTO & Sick Leave Paid Unpaid (lost revenue)
Insurance & Benefits Subsidized by employer 100% Out of pocket
Risk & Security Severance, UI eligibility Terminated at will, no UI

How much more should a contractor make than an employee?

As a baseline rule, a contractor should charge 30% to 50% more per hour than an equivalent employee's hourly rate. This markup is necessary to cover self-employment taxes, health insurance premiums, retirement contributions, and the unpaid time you spend on holidays, sick days, and business administration. If the premium is less than 15%, the stability of the employee role is mathematically more valuable.

The Reality of PTO, Benefits, Tax & Risk

Unlike salaried employees, independent workers take on the full burden of business logistics. Every week of vacation or illness means zero revenue. When comparing offers, remember that an employee's $100k represents a total compensation package likely worth $130k+, whereas a contractor's $100k represents gross revenue before business expenses and higher taxes.
